Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to set aside time each day, but with 13 online courses, it felt impossible to keep track of everything. I'm someone who learns best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often spend time practicing problems or working through examples rather than just reading. I aimed for about 20 hours a week, but a lot of that time felt unproductive because I was just trying to figure out what to prioritize.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched certain courses, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble to catch up. With my extracurriculars taking up time too, I couldn't really stick to a consistent schedule. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied, not any kind of regular routine.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I got a notification about something being due or when I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any updates or announcements. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more often because I wanted to see my progress and earn those points, even if it didn't translate to better grades.
Q4: For a little while, checking the LMS became kind of automatic, especially when the gamified elements were introduced. I'd log in to see my badges or check the leaderboard. But that routine faded when I realized I was still falling behind on actual assignments. It became more of a habit to check rather than a habit to actually engage deeply.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could pause and rewind when I needed to understand something better. I watched over 200 minutes of video content because that format worked well with how I learn. The discussion boards were also helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways than the instructor did.
Q6: I often ignored the readings and some of the supplementary materials because there was just too much content across all my courses. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but rarely did. Some resources felt redundant or too theoretical when I really needed practical examples to understand things.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on exams or assignments. If I could actually do something with my hands or work through a problem step by step, that felt valuable. Activities that were just about absorbing information passively didn't feel as worthwhile to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When I was already struggling to complete required work, doing extra stuff seemed pointless even if it might help me learn. The gamified tasks blurred this line a bit because they felt optional but also gave me some sense of accomplishment, even if small.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them on my phone or printed them out. I'd work through problems on paper because that's how I learn best. I also used YouTube videos and other external sites when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my laptop.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through practice problems on paper or discussing concepts with classmates in person, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours trying to understand something using my own notes and external resources. The LMS only saw me when I logged in, not when I was actually struggling through the material.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs once and then never open them in the LMS again. I'd study from the downloaded versions for days, marking them up and making notes. To the system, it probably looked like I only glanced at them briefly.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed down on me. I had multiple exams and assignments due across different courses, plus my extracurricular commitments. I felt really sad and overwhelmed, like I was drowning and couldn't catch up no matter what I did. My attendance dropped because I was trying to study instead of going to classes that felt less urgent.
Q13: The workload was crushing with 13 courses. I was constantly fatigued and confused about what to prioritize. When I was stressed, I'd sometimes just stare at the LMS without actually doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars made it worse because I couldn't give up those commitments either.
Q14: There were definitely times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. I'd technically be watching something but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other deadlines or just feeling too tired to process anything. The engagement looked real on the surface but I wasn't actually absorbing the material.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was that I'm a kinesthetic learner and some assignments just felt like busywork that wouldn't help me actually understand. I kept telling myself I'd do them later, but later kept getting pushed back.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, sometimes completing things at the last minute. But with so many courses, I'd often miss deadlines entirely because I was focused on something else that was due first. My 22% completion rate reflects how often I just couldn't keep up.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next assignment. But honestly, by the time feedback came, I was already behind on new things. The gamified task feedback was more immediate and that helped keep me engaged in the moment, even if it didn't improve my overall performance.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming because there was so much content spread across different modules. I never felt like I had a clear path through everything. The gamified elements helped give some structure to my engagement, but the underlying course organization still felt chaotic to me.
Q19: Having videos made engagement easier because I could learn at my own pace. But the sheer volume of content made it harder. I also struggled because the course wasn't very hands-on, and I really need to physically do things to learn. More interactive elements or practical exercises would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think sometimes students are trying to engage but they're lost or overwhelmed. They might be clicking around looking for something that makes sense to them, or they're checking in hoping to find motivation. High activity doesn't mean productive activity, especially when you're stressed and don't know what to prioritize.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some students might be really efficient or studying offline. I spent a lot of time learning outside the LMS that didn't show up in my logs. If someone has good external resources or strong study groups, they might not need to be on the platform much to do well.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show what you're actually thinking or understanding. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with friends, the moments of genuine insight or confusion. They also can't tell the difference between focused engagement and mindless scrolling.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and want to figure things out, when you're actively thinking rather than passively watching. For me, it's when I'm doing something with my hands, working through problems, feeling like I'm making progress.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad and overwhelmed I've been feeling, or how hard I've been trying despite my poor results. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline or the peer study sessions that actually helped me understand things. It also wouldn't show that I'm resilient and haven't given up, even though my grades suggest otherwise.
